Practices Commission (1-990) 747 F.Supp. 58o (trSEIUrt) {/ ,u=
dissolved. consequentl-y, the provisions of Proposition 73
providing fiscal year contribution linits and, in Part, section
85304, $rhich bans candidate to candidate transfers, are no longer
being enforced. However, the contribution lirnits and transfer ban
as applied to special elections continue to be in force.
OUESTIONS
1. May a group of candidates jointly control the Fanily
congress and conference on the Preservation of the Fanily?
2. May campaign funds be used to contribute to the Faroily
congress and Conference on the Preservation of the Family where
the orqanization either qualifies as a committee or as a nonprofit
corporat i on?
CONCLUS IONS
1. If the conference receives contri.butions in excess of
$1,ooo j,n a caLendar year, the conference would be a joint
candidate controlled committee. Since a candidate may have only
one campaign bank account and cornmittee for each election, a
candidate cannot control a campaign committee and be a controlling
member of the conference under the Act. A nonprofit corporatj'on
formed consistent with Regutation 18217 is presumed not to be a
controlled cornmittee.
2. Campaign funds rnay be donated to the Farnily congress and
conference on the Preservation of the Family whether the
organization is a committee or a nonprofit corporation. Surplus
funds may not be transferred to a candidate controlled cornnittee
formed in california. 
Hov/ever, surpl-us funds rnay be donated to a
nonprof it organization.
FACTS
In 1988, Republican l-egislators formed the Republican Family
Task Force to directly create legislation to be introduced in the
Legislature. 
The group was renamed the Conference on the
Preservation of the Family in 1990 (the "conferencerr) . 
The
conference is controlled by 23 nenbers of the Legislature, with
input from a public advisory cornrnittee nade up of 10o or nore
non Iegis l-ators .
1t on september 25, L99O, the United states District Court issued
an order j,n the SElg case vhich invalidated portions of the Act
which were added by Proposition 73 in June of 1988. However, on
September 28 | L990, the federal court stayed execution of the
order with respect to l-egislative candidates until there was a
final judgment that the contribution lirnits of Proposition 68 were
not effective. 
Please note, the decision in SEIU is being
appea led .


Our File No. 
■-9■-03■
Page 3
In September of 1988 the Task Force heLd four pubLic hearings
throughout the state to solicit public input on the role of
government with respect to the family. 
Then, based on testimony
ieceived at the hearings, the rnernbers of the conference introduced
35 bills deating with farnily issues.
In 1990, a decision hras made by the conference to sponsor a
state-wide public conference vhich has been titled the Family
congress. To help fund the event, each legislator in the
conierence has aqreed to contribute some of his or her canpaign
funds to the conference. The conference plans on setting up a
joint bank account into which these funds will be deposited and
frorn which expenditures for the event will be made. You stated
that neither the conference nor the account would be used to
support or oppose any candidate for office.
ANALYSIS
1. Controlled Connittees
section 82ol-6 defines rrcontrolled committeel as follows:
rrcontrolled committeetr means a cornmittee which
is controlled dj-rectly or indirectly by a candidate
or state measure proponent or which acts jointly
with a candidate, controlled committee or state
measure proponent in connection srith the naking of
expenditures. A candidate or state neasure
proponent controls a committee if he, his agent or
any other comrnittee he controls has a significant
infLuence on the actions or decisions of the
committee .
Thus, Section 82016 describes thro rrrays in which the
conference may become a controlled conrnittee. First, a candidate
or multiple candidates might exert siqnificant influence on the
actions or decisions of the conference. Where candidates are
votinq members of the conference's l-eadership, they are presumed
to be controlling candidates since they exercise significant
influence on the actions or decisions of the conference.
(Ferquson Advice Letter, No. A-86-044, copy enclosed. )
second, a candidate or multiple candidates may act in concert
with the conference in rnaking carnpaign expenditures. By
coordinating the expenditures, the candidate is in effect
botstering his or her campaign for office by use of the
conference. Under such circumstances, the conference would also
be a controlled committee.
Section 82025 defines "expendituretr as any palaltent,
forgiveness of a loan, payment of a loan by a third party, or any
enforceable prornise to make a payment, unless it is cl,ear from the
surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political


Our File No. I-9■
-03■
Page 4
purposes. A payment is made for political 
purposes if it is for
the- purpose of influencing or atternpting to influence the action
of the voters for or against the nomination or election of a
candidate.or candidates, or the gualification or passage of any
,easur".5/ 
(Regulation ]-a225, copy enclosed.)
The conference rrrould be control.fed by 23 leqislators.5/
cl-early, if the conference gualifies as a I'committeerr the
conference would meet the definition of rrcontrolled cornnittee'r in
Section 82016. According to your facts, the activities of the
conference will be to create legislation and not to support or
oppose candidates. A "cornmittee" is defined as folloers:
rrcommitteett means any person or combination of
persons who directLy or indirectly does any of the
fo I lowing :
(a) Receives contributions totaling one
thousand dollars ($1,000) or nore in a calendar
year .
(b) Makes independent expenditures totaling
one thousand dollars (91,000) or more in a calendar
year; or
(c) Makes contributions totaling ten thousand
dollars (S10,0oo) or more in a calendar year to or
at the behest of candidates or committees.
A person or combination of persons that
becomes a committee shall retain its status as a
comrnittee until such time as that status is
terminated pursuant to Section S 84214.
Section A2O73.
5/ You have also asked about the significance of the definition
of "political 
purposerr in the various sections of the Act dealingl
with carnpaigns. In implementing the Act the Commission has found
that it is necessary to look at the goals and intent of the Act as
a rrhol,e, rather than closely scrutinizing the components of
whatever definition may be applicable in a given situation. 
(rn
re witt (]-976) I FPPC ops. 1, copy enclosed.) Thus, the
definition of "political 
purposerr may differ dependingr on the
context in which it is applied.
6/ Mernbership alone in the conference would not make the
conference a candidate controlled conmittee, provided no candidate
exerts significant influence over the actions or decisions of the
conference gI acts in concert r,rith the conference to influence the
voting public concerning any candidate or bal-lot measure. (Madden
Advice Letter, No. A-85-197, copy enclosed.)


Our Fi■e No. I-9■-03■
Page 5
cenerally, a pa)rment received by a candidate j.s a
contribution unless it is clear from surrounding circumstances
that it was received for personal purposes unrelated to his or her
candidacy or status as an officeholder. 
(Regulation 18215, copy
enclosed). Thus, payments to an organization controlled by
candidates could be considered contributions. 
when sufficient
contributions are received, the organization would qualify as a
committee.
Section 89510, added by Proposition 73, provides that all
contributions or loans nade to a candidate, or to the candidate's
controtl-ed committee shall be deposited in a single campaign bank
account. The commission has interpreted this to mean that A
account and one contro■
■ed committee for each campaiqn.
(Regulation :-852Li Riddle Advice Letter, No. A-88-409, copies
enclosed. )
consequently, a candidate cannot control a campaign committee
and be a controlling member of the conference consistent with the
provisions of the Act if the conference is a controll-ed committee.
However, a candidate may fund the qctivity you have described from
his or her campaign bank account.S/ Expenditures for purposes you
have described would generally be permitted since the expenditures
appear to be reasonablv related to a potitical, 
legislative, 
or
governmental purpose and confer no substantial personal benefit on
the candidate. (Section 89511; Section 89512.)
Further, the candidate may accept transfers from other
candidates for this purpose. on September 25, L99o' the transfer
ban of Section 85304 l^ras found to be unconstitutional. 
Thus,
transfers of campaign funds between candidates are now generally
permitted. 9/
7 / The connission has created a narrorr exception with respect to
candidate controlled ball-ot measure conmittees which is not an
issue in this letter. 
(olson Advice Letter, No. A-89-363, copy
enclosed. )
8/ where the candidate is within 3 nonths of an election for
r^/hich he or she has fifed a candidate intention statement (Form
5ol-); a declaration of candj.dacy or nomination papers; or any
other documents necessary to be listed on the bal-l-ot as a
candidate for any state or local office, Regulation 18525 (copy
encfosed) requj,res that pa)anents for mass rnailings be made from
the appropriate campaign bank account for election to a future
term of office.
>t However, the contribution limits and transfer ban of
Proposition 73 continue to be operative with respect to any
special election or special runoff election, r^/hether locaI or
statewide .


our File No. 
■-9■-03■
Page 6
However, Section 8951,0(b) of the Act continues to require
that aff contributions deposited into the campaign account be held
in trust for expenses associated with the election of the
candidate to the specific office which the candidate has stated
that he or she intends to seek or for expenses associated with
hotding that office. 
Thus, while the transfer of funds is
permitted, the transfer must stil1 serve a purpose associated with
th" tr..r=i"rrer's 
election or office.lo/
2. Exception For Nonprofit organizations
Regulation L8217 (copy enclosed) perrnits a candidate or
multiple candidates to set up an organization as a nonprofit
corpoiation without the corporation becoming an additional
controlled committee of the candidate. rrl
(a) A nonprofit organization, as defined in
subdivision (f), shall be considered a controlled
comrnittee, if both of the following apply:
(1) A candidate, his or her agent, or
any conrnittee he or she controls, exercises
significant influence over the actions and
deiisions of the organization, or acts jointly
with the organj-zation in connection with the
naking of expenditures.
(2') The organization qualifies as a com-
mittee under Government Code section 82013(a),
purposes. For purposes of this regulation, an
organization is rroperated for political
puiposesl if either of the following applies:
(A) The organization receives or
expends funds for the purpose of
influencing or attempting to influence
the action of the voters for or against
1o/ where campaign funds become surplus carnpaign funds,.the
personal use 1iw iurther Iirnits their expenditure. . Section 89519
permits the use of surp).us carnpaign funds for only the purPoses
iiiiea 
in that section-. Transiert to other California candidates
is not a specified permitted use. Thus, while carnpaign funds may
be transfeired to a candidate for this purpose, surplus carnpaign
funds may not. Please note, however, that carnpaign funds only
become sirrptus upon the defeat of a candidate or when the
candidate leaves office. 
(Section 89519.)
LL/ ttNonprof it organizationrr means an organization which is
characterized as an exernpt organization under Section 501 of the
Internal Revenue code, but which is not an organization formed or
.*i=ti.,g primarily for political purposes' (Regul-ation 182L7(f) ')


Our Fi■e NO. I-9■-03■
Page 7
the nomination or election of a candidate
or the qualification or passage of any
neasure .
(B) The organization makes
contributions to candidates or their
controlled conrnittees.
The purpose of subdivision (a) (2) of Regnrlation 18217 is to
avoid classifying a nonpolitical nonprofit orqanization as a
controLfed conmittee merelv because donations are received at the
behest of candidates. Regulation 18217 also provides for a
presurnption that a nonprofit organization is not a controlled
Lonrnittee if certain criteria are met. (see, Regulation 18217.)
Thus, candidates rnay establish nonprofit organizations
consistent with Regulation 18217 with sorne certainty that they
will not be viewed as controlled conmittees. (See, stathan Advice
Letter, No. I-90-246, copy enclosed.) Further, candidates may
donate campaign funds to the nonprofit organization if: 
. (1) the
nonprofit orginization is a bona fide charitable, educational,
civj,c, reJ,igious or similar tax-exempt organization; (2) the
expenditure is reasonably related to a political, 
legislative or
gTovernrnentat purpose; and, (3) the expenditure will not have a
iaterial financial effect on the candidate, his or her inrnediate
family, or the candidate's carnpaign treasurer.12l 
(section
895r-5. )
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact ne at (916) 322-5904.
Sincere■y′
Scott Hal■abrin
Acting Cenera■ Counse■

lzt 
The test applied to the donation of surplus campaign funds to
nonprofit corporations is substantially the same except that there
need not be the requisite po1itical,, legislative or governmental
purpose behind the transfer to the nonprofit. 
(Section 89519(b).)

sections.question:
1. May a group of candidates jointly control the Family congress and conference on the Preservation of the Family?
2. May campaign funds be used to contribute to the Family congress and Conference on the Preservation of the Family where the organization either qualifies as a committee or as a nonprofit corporation?
sections.conclusion:
1. If the conference receives contributions in excess of $1,000 in a calendar year, the conference would be a joint candidate controlled committee. Since a candidate may have only one campaign bank account and committee for each election, a candidate cannot control a campaign committee and be a controlling member of the conference under the Act. A nonprofit corporation formed consistent with Regulation 18217 is presumed not to be a controlled committee.
2. Campaign funds may be donated to the Family congress and Conference on the Preservation of the Family whether the organization is a committee or a nonprofit corporation. Surplus funds may not be transferred to a candidate controlled committee formed in California. However, surplus funds may be donated to a nonprofit organization.

sections.question_synthetic: Can a group of legislative candidates jointly control an organization called the Family Congress and Conference on the Preservation of the Family, and may campaign funds be donated to it whether it operates as a committee or nonprofit corporation?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: If the conference receives over $1,000 in contributions annually, it becomes a joint candidate-controlled committee, and candidates cannot simultaneously control both it and their own campaign committees. Campaign funds may be donated to the conference whether it operates as a committee or nonprofit corporation, though surplus funds cannot be transferred to candidate-controlled committees formed in California.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document contains OCR artifacts and scanning errors affecting some text clarity. Questions and Conclusions sections clearly delineated. File number I-91-031, dated February 28, 1991. Response to Jeff Evans, Assembly Republican Caucus regarding Political Reform Act compliance.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. May a group of candidates jointly control the Family congress and conference on the Preservation of the Family?
2. May campaign funds be used to contribute to the Family congress and Conference on the Preservation of the Family where the organization either qualifies as a committee or as a nonprofit corporation?

CONCLUSION: 1. If the conference receives contributions in excess of $1,000 in a calendar year, the conference would be a joint candidate controlled committee. Since a candidate may have only one campaign bank account and committee for each election, a candidate cannot control a campaign committee and be a controlling member of the conference under the Act. A nonprofit corporation formed consistent with Regulation 18217 is presumed not to be a controlled committee.
2. Campaign funds may be donated to the Family congress and Conference on the Preservation of the Family whether the organization is a committee or a nonprofit corporation. Surplus funds may not be transferred to a candidate controlled committee formed in California. However, surplus funds may be donated to a nonprofit organization.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises Republican legislators that a legislative conference controlled by 23 state legislators and a joint bank account receiving campaign contributions would constitute a controlled committee if it receives over $1,000 annually, but campaign funds may still be donated to the organization whether structured as a committee or nonprofit corporation.
